Dataset Name: Direction de l'Évaluation, de la Prospective et de la Performance - Directorate of Evaluation, Forecasting, and Performance - Panel 2011 (entrants CP)
Short Description: The French DEPP Panel 2011 contains longitudinal and administrative data following 15,200 first graders for 9 years. It includes detailed information on students' and families' socio-demographic characteristics, their cultural and material environments, and the development of students’ cognitive skills over time. Data is available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: Yes, simplified information for both parents
Parental Education Level (ISCED): No
Teacher Information Type: Administrative (provided by the institution or others)
Teacher Information Linkability: Not linkable to the ones of students

Dataset Name: Étude Longitudinale Française depuis l'Enfance - French Longitudinal Study of Children
Short Description: ELFE contains data on over 18,000 children tracked from birth into adulthood as part of a national longitudinal study in France. It covers multiple aspects of children's lives, including education, family dynamics, environmental conditions, and health. Cognitive and developmental assessments are conducted during early childhood education, as well as in first and fourth grades. Data is accessible upon request.

Dataset Name: Mehrsprachigkeitsentwicklung im Zeitverlauf
Short Description: MEZ provides data on two parallel cohorts of German students, followed annually through either seventh to ninth grade or ninth to eleventh grade. Students were interviewed and tested linguistically. Data is fully accessible.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Sample Level (Specified): Baden-Württemberg, Bremen, Hamburg, Hesse, Lower Saxony, North Rhine Westphalia, Rhineland-Palatine, Schleswig-Holstein
Sampling Weights/Criteria: The study sample was compiled according to defined criteria, so that no random sampling was used, but rather a criteria-based selection. Schools were required that had a sufficiently high number of students with a Russian and Turkish language background and that offered the foreign language courses tested by MEZ. As the recruitment of schools proved difficult, the criteria were adjusted during the recruitment process. This included, among other things, the successive expansion of the catchment area from the original three federal states to eight federal states. The selection of potential schools was based on information from the statistical offices of the respective federal states. At the first measurement point, 72 schools with 1,875 willing students participated in the study. After the first measurement point, the absolute number of participants achieved corresponded to the targeted 1,800 students, but the sample showed a skewed distribution to the detriment of German-Russian and German-Turkish-speaking students. In addition, students from lower educational levels were not as well represented in the sample as hoped. To ensure the expected results, the sample was expanded again at the second measurement point. In the second approach, schools that had already inquired but had not participated at the first measurement point were included. Second, schools that had already participated in the first survey but had a low participation rate among their students were contacted. In both cases, it was assumed that there was potentially a large number of students who met the sample criteria. From then on, 78 schools with 2,103 students willing to participate were included in the sample (IEA Hamburg 2017b).  Subsequently, logit models are used to analyze the sample dropouts of the two MEZ-2 surveys (MZP 5 and MZP 6) for selectivity. The results reveal systematic, albeit rather small, effects of an increased risk of dropping out of the original MEZ gross sample for participants with low or unknown socioeconomic status, for participants in previously non-gymnastic programs, for participants in the older starting cohort, for male participants, and for participants with a German-Turkish language background.
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: Yes, detailed information (e.g. ISCO code) for both parents
Parental Education Level (ISCED): No

Dataset Name: Inklusion in und nach der Sekundarstufe in Deutschland
Short Description: INSIDE provides longitudinal data on more than 1,000 German secondary school students, focusing on school inclusion. Data is available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Sampling Weights/Criteria: A random sample of 5.500 schools was drawn. Those schools were asked for information on the joint learning of students with and without special educational needs. Information from 1360 schools was obtained, of which 1014 schools actively participated in the survey. Based on the information and the official data, 745 schools were selected at which at least three SEN students were taught in the areas of “learning” and/or “emotional and social development” in grade 5. So, it was highly probable that at least one of the three students would attend the following school year. Moreover, according to the official statistics, an additional 1.896 schools were chosen from the official data that were likely to teach SEN students in grade 6. To even further enlarge the sample. Information from the sample of 5500 schools was drawn upon again, and 1813 schools were selected that had already been asked to participate but did not participate in the survey. In total, the school-level sample had 4455 schools. However, in the next school year, the schools were asked about the number of sixth-grade SEN students, and the net sample was reduced to 231 schools. From these schools, a net sample of 3899 students, of these 519 students had special needs. Moreover, 2222 parents agree to participate. The second cohort was sampled using a different approach. Schools that had already been contacted for the first cohort who did not answer were approached. Moreover, schools that already participated in the first cohort were asked. The gross sample included 2179 schools. The net sample of the second cohort was 51 schools. 755 students agreed to participate, of whom 183 students had special needs. 400 parents agree to participate. However, due to COVID-19 and panel attrition, the final sample will be smaller.

Dataset Name: COVID Social Mobility and Opportunities
Short Description: COSMO is a major national longitudinal study which is examining the unequal impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic and its aftermath on the life chances of a generation of young people. Spanning two waves it tracks over 13,000 students in England capturing data on educational inequality, wellbeing, and life chances amid COVID-19 and the cost-of-living crisis. Wave 1 and 2 data are accessible via the UK Data Service.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Sampling Weights/Criteria: A random sample of students but with a boost of those groups: those eligible for Free School Meals at any point over the last six years, from the six main ethnic minority groups, and those that speak English as an additional language. 12.154 students were interviewed for the main sample. There was further a Sutton Trust Boost sample with 2.000 students who were eligible for free school meals in the last 6 years and the top 33% in the combined reading, maths, and GPS KS2 score
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: Yes, detailed information (e.g. ISCO code) for both parents

Sampling Weights/Criteria: nationally representative quantitative study of 189 primary schools, and in-depth case studies of a sub-sample of 13 primary schools

Dataset Name: Children of Immigrants Longitudinal Survey in the Netherlands
Short Description: CILSNL is a 7-year longitudinal study launched in 2010, following a national sample of 14-year-olds in the Netherlands, including both immigrant-origin students and a native reference group. It tracks educational progress, integration, values, and social participation across all school tracks. Data are accessible to researchers through CILS4EU application procedures.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Sampling Weights/Criteria: All schools in the country are invited to participate in the statistical operations taking place in this context, but not all register (the registration rate varies approximately between 75% and 90%). The survey is voluntary and requires authorization, so not all students in registered schools complete the survey (the response rate for students in registered schools varies depending on the survey, approximately between 55% and 70%). If we consider the response rate for the total school population for the target group, it varies between 50% and 60%). In any case, I recommend consulting the more detailed publications for all available surveys on the DGEEC website: https://www.dgeec.medu.pt/art/6499de939eff36f307f07bdc/65524fc0455255473193d2bd/65524fef455255473193d2bf/652ec184bd5c2b00958292b5

Dataset Name: Estonian Education Information System
Short Description: The Estonian Education Information System (EHIS) is a national register established in 2004 that compiles data across all school tracks. It includes information on students, teachers, institutions, curricula, diplomas, and educational support. Data are accessible upon registration and may be subject to fees.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer with a specific interface
Student Information Type: Administrative (provided by the institution or others)
Parental Occupation: Not clear
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Not clear

Dataset Name: Eksamite Infosüsteemi (Examinations Information System)
Short Description: The Examinations Information System (EIS) is Estonia’s national administrative system for collecting exam data from 9th and 12th grade students. It covers the years 2015 to 2024, with most exams missing in 2020. The system provides centralized data on student assessments. Access conditions are not publicly specified.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer with a specific interface
Parental Occupation: Not clear
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Not clear

Dataset Name: Centralised Exam Results
Short Description: Latvia’s Centralised Exam Results system compiles national data on examination outcomes across all education levels and school types, from basic to higher education. It includes gymnasiums, vocational institutions, distance learning, and more. Anonymised aggregated data are available without restriction from the data provider’s website.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without restrictions
Student Information Type: Administrative (provided by the institution or others)
Parental Occupation: No
Parental Education Level (ISCED): No

Dataset Name: NACIONALINIAI MOKINIŲ PASIEKIMŲ TYRIMAI (National Survey of Student Achievement )
Short Description: The National Survey of Student Achievement (NSSA) is a national assessment system in Lithuania, collecting data from 2nd, 4th, 6th, and 8th grade students. Conducted every other year, it includes only assessment data since 2012. Covering primary and middle secondary education, anonymised data are available without restrictions.
Data Collection Frequency: Every other year
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without restrictions
Parental Occupation: Yes, simplified information for both parents
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Yes, simplified ISCED for both parents

Dataset Name: Sistema de Informação do Ministério da Educação e Ciência
Short Description: The Sistema de Informação do Ministério da Educação e Ciência (MISI) is an administrative dataset in Portugal collecting grades and exam results at all levels from pre-school to secondary education. Established in 2006, it is updated at least annually. Microdata access is only possible on-site at the facilities of the data provider.

Dataset Name: Escola 360
Short Description: Escola 360 (E360) is a centralized student management system established in 2014 by Portugal’s Ministry of Education. It covers all levels from pre-school to secondary education. Microdata access requires physical presence at the facilities of DGEEC, the data owner, and is subject to strict access conditions.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Access to Micro Data: No Access or Limited Access Under Specific Conditions

Dataset Name: Sistema Integrado de Informação e Gestão da Oferta Educativa e Formativa
Short Description: The Sistema Integrado de Informação e Gestão da Oferta Educativa e Formativa (SIGO) is an administrative dataset in Portugal, active since 2000. It covers all school grades, from primary to post-secondary education and adult training. Microdata access requires on-site presence at DGEEC and linkage to other administrative data is restricted to data owners.

Dataset Name: Growing Up in Ireland, Infant Cohort
Short Description: Growing Up in Ireland – Cohort ’08 is a national longitudinal study tracking over 11,000 children from 9 months old, beginning in 2008. Follow-ups were conducted at ages 3, 5, 7/8 (postal), 9, and 13, with work at age 17 underway. It covers multiple life domains, including education. Anonymised microdata are available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Every four year or more
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: No
Parental Education Level (ISCED): No
Household Information Type: Administrative (provided by the institution or others)

Dataset Name: Growing up in Ireland, Child cohort
Short Description: Child Cohort (IE) - The Growing Up in Ireland – Child Cohort began in 2008 with 8,500 nine-year-olds. Data were collected from children, parents, teachers, principals, and others involved in their care. Follow-ups occurred at ages 13, 17/18, and 20, with data from age 25 currently being prepared. Anonymised microdata are available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Every four year or more
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: No
Parental Education Level (ISCED): No
Teacher Information Linkability: Linkable to the ones of students

Dataset Name: Growing Up in Scotland, Birth cohort 1
Short Description: Growing Up in Scotland (GUS) is a major longitudinal study tracking the lives of children and their families. Birth Cohort 1, launched in 2005, includes 2,858 children born between June 2002 and May 2003. Four waves of data were collected from just under age 3 to just under age 6. Anonymised data are available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Every other year
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: Not clear
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Yes, full ISCED for both parents

Dataset Name: Growing Up in Scotland, Birth cohort 2
Short Description: Growing Up in Scotland’s Birth Cohort 1 follows 5,217 children born between June 2004 and May 2005. Data were collected from age 10 months to 17 years, with the latest wave at age 19/20 underway in 2025. The study includes linked health and education records. Anonymised microdata are available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Every other year
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: Not clear
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Yes, full ISCED for both parents

Dataset Name: Growing Up in Scotland, Child cohort
Short Description: Birth Cohort 2 (UK) - Birth Cohort 2 of Growing Up in Scotland includes around 6,127 children born between March 2010 and February 2011. Data were collected at ages 10 months, just under 3, and just under 5. The final sweep took place in 2015/16, and no further data collection is planned. Anonymised data are available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: On a personal computer without a specific interface
Parental Occupation: Not clear
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Yes, full ISCED for both parents

Dataset Name: Transformation des Sekundarschulsystems und akademische Karrieren (2002)
Short Description: TOSCA-2002 is a longitudinal study examining the educational outcomes of 4,730 upper secondary graduates from 90 general and 59 vocational high schools in Baden-Württemberg during the 2001/02 school year. Follow-up surveys were conducted every two years until 2012, with a final wave in 2016. Data include information on school reform impacts, core subject assessments (Math or English), and school leadership characteristics. Data cover transitions to higher education and vocational training. Access is available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Every other year
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: Not clear
Parental Occupation: Yes, detailed information (e.g. ISCO code) for both parents
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Yes, full ISCED for both parents

Dataset Name: Transformation des Sekundarschulsystems und akademische Karrieren (2006)
Short Description: TOSCA-2006 is the second cohort of the TOSCA study, following 5,016 upper secondary graduates from over 150 Gymnasiums in Baden-Württemberg, first surveyed in 2006. Follow-up postal surveys were conducted every two years until 2012, with a final wave in 2016. The study tracks educational and career pathways over a 10-year period. Data access is available upon request.
Data Collection Frequency: Every other year
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: Not clear
Parental Occupation: Yes, detailed information (e.g. ISCO code) for both parents
Parental Education Level (ISCED): Yes, full ISCED for both parents

Short Description: Admin4 is a linked administrative panel dataset developed by KRTK covering all pupils from 2007 to 2021. It integrates data from multiple administrative sources to track individuals’ educational and labor market trajectories over time. The dataset enables longitudinal analysis but is not publicly accessible; data access is available upon request and subject to approval.

Short Description: Admin3 is a linked administrative panel dataset developed by KRTK in Hungary, similar to Admin4. It covers all pupils and tracks educational and labour market trajectories using data from multiple administrative sources. The dataset allows longitudinal analysis from 2003 to 2017. Microdata access is restricted and subject to formal request and approval.

Dataset Name: PRIMary to Secondary education (PRIMS), Cohort 1
Short Description: PRIMS  Cohort 1 is a 1.5-year longitudinal study conducted in the Netherlands from 2020 to 2021. It focuses on the role of peer relationships during the transition from primary to secondary education, aiming to understand how these relationships influence educational inequalities and students' social integration. Data collection involved surveys administered to students in their final year of primary school and again after transitioning to secondary school. The dataset includes information on peer networks, educational aspirations, and academic performance. Access to the data is restricted and will be made available to researchers under certain conditions in 2024.
Data Collection Frequency: Yearly (or more than once per year)
Access to Micro Data: No Access or Limited Access Under Specific Conditions
Constraints for Data Download and Manage: Not clear
Parental Occupation: Yes, detailed information (e.g. ISCO code) for both parents
Parental Education Level (ISCED): No
Teacher Information Linkability: Linkable to the ones of students
